title: practical english usage - Part 120
content:
The Times The Washington Post The  names of newspapers usually have the.

19 newspapers         and   magazines but                                not Mont Blanc (     the Mont Blanc) The Matterhorn (= Das Matterhorn) The Meije (= La Meije) mountain names, except those beginning Le Mont.

But definite articles are usually translated in the English versions of European Everest Kilimanjaro Snowdon Table Mountain Names of single mountains vary. Most have no article.

(The) East Oxford Community Centre.  (The) Newbury School of English.

With the names of less important institutions, usage varies.

Birmingham Airport Cheltenham Football Club Salisbury Cathedral Manchester City Council not Hull  Station (     the Hu ll Station) not Oxford University (     the Oxfbrd-University) a town, when the title begins with the town name.

The is unusual in the titles of the principal public buildings and organisations of with no article: (The) Ukraine, (The) Lebanon, (The) Gambia, (The) Sudan.

are a few countries whose names used to have the, but are now normally used States). Note also the Netherlands, and its seat of government The Hague. There state, union (e.g. the People's Republic of China, the United Kingdom, the United Exceptions: places whose name is (or contains) a common noun like republic, •    lakes (Lake Michigan) •    streets (New Street, Willow Road) •    towns (Oxford) Berkshire, Westphalia) •    continents, countries, states, counties, departments, etc (Africa, Brazil, Texas, We usually use no article with: •    most museums and art galleries (the British Museum;  the Frick) •    most cinemas and theatres (the Odeon; the Playhouse) •     most hotels (the Grand Hotel) •    deserts (the Sahara) •    rivers (the Rhine) •    island groups (the West Indies) •    mountain groups (the Himalayas) •    seas (the Atlantic) We   use the with these kinds of place names: 18  place    names sixty pence a kilo thirty miles an hour twice a week Alan is used to relate one measuring unit to another.

grammar •  143   possessive determiners: my, your, etc not How's that brother of yours? (     .. . that your brother?)         -> not A friend of mine has just invited me to Italy, (     A my friend.. .) '... of mine' structure (►    177).

If we  want to use a/an or this, that, etc with a possessive, we  use the not She's lost her keys, (     ... the her keys.) My, your, etc are not used with other determiners like the, a/an or this.

3 not   used    with    other     determiners An orphan is a child whose parents are dead. Whose bicycle is that?

It's easy to lose one's temper when one is criticised.

pronouns.

One's (►   181.5) and whose (►  235, 628) are also possessive determiners/ 2 one's     and   whose they are not used as determiners.

Note that mine, yours, etc (►   176) are also pronouns (of a different kind), but possession.

Like all 'possessive' words and structures, they can express various ideas besides in older grammars and dictionaries.

They  are not adjectives, though they are sometimes called 'possessive adjectives' They  are used at the beginning of noun phrases, and function as determiners.

‘ the children's plans’ .

brother'; their plans means for example 'those people's plans' or possessive noun phrases: my younger brother means 'the speaker's younger My, your, his, her, its, our and their are pronouns, because they stand for 1  What    kind    of  words     are   they?
